title: Collect Data — Get Custody
intro:
STEP 3 OF 8 · MODULE 3

Collect Data — Get Custody

Mission: Get the vehicle into our bay. Separate the customer from the car. Document the true concern.

OBJECTIVE
GET CUSTODY
TOOL
BOOM BOARD + RO
PRIMARY KPI
INQUIRY → RO %
FAILURE MODE
SELF-DIAGNOSIS

sections.title: The Psychology of Custody
sections.html:
The Psychology of Custody

The single most under-valued asset in the shop is the car in our bay. We cannot inspect a car we don’t have. We cannot sell repairs on a car we don’t have. We cannot build a relationship through a phone — only across a counter and a coffee. Custody is the gateway to every other objective in this system. Every move in Step 3 is designed to move the vehicle from “maybe” to “in the bay.”

The corollary: the second the vehicle is in the bay, we have also done a quieter but equally important thing — we have separated the customer from the vehicle. The customer is now in the waiting room or in an Uber. They are no longer staring at the dashboard. Their emotional attachment to the symptom decays. Logical decisions become possible. Separation is what enables education.

sections.title: Concern vs. Symptom — The Most Important Distinction in Service
sections.html:
Concern vs. Symptom — The Most Important Distinction in Service

Customers describe symptoms. Professionals diagnose concerns. The single biggest source of wasted shop time is taking a customer’s self-diagnosis at face value. “I need new brakes” is a symptom statement — the concern might be a wheel bearing, a stuck caliper, or a low-friction tire. Our job is to convert symptom language into concern language, gently, without making the customer feel stupid.

Figure 3.1 · The diagnosis funnel — customer language converts to professional language at each gate

sections.title: Question Sequences That Get Custody
sections.html:
Question Sequences That Get Custody

The right question sequence walks the customer up a small hill of yeses until the “bring it in” ask feels like the next obvious step rather than a separate decision.

SequencePurposeExample
1. Acknowledge concernValidates the customer’s reality.“Oh wow, when you’re experiencing [concern]…”
2. Imply expertise requiredPositions inspection as the only honest answer.“…it’s always best to have a certified tech inspect.”
3. Assume the appointmentSkips the should/shouldn’t fork.“Is now a good time?”
4. East-West closeCollapses scheduling infinity to binary.“9:15 or 10:45?”
5. Custody hand-offLocks the appointment with action.“Stay with me while I get you in our system.”

sections.title: The Boom Board — Custody’s Best Friend
sections.html:
The Boom Board — Custody’s Best Friend

The boom board (intake clipboard, tablet, or shop-management capture form) is the physical artifact of custody. It signals professionalism, captures the concern in writing, and gives the customer something to participate in. Walk-ins fill it out. Phone-ins get it built by the advisor as they talk. No vehicle enters the bay without one.

STOPNo boom board, no bay.

If a tech is performing work on a vehicle that does not have a documented concern in the system, the whole sales process has skipped Step 3. Stop the work. Document. Then proceed. This single discipline protects the shop from miscommunication and the customer from surprise.

sections.title: Mastery Check — Custody Certified
sections.html:
Mastery Check — Custody Certified

LEVEL 3 · CUSTODY MASTERY

  • Converted the customer’s symptom language to a documented concern.
  • Did not accept self-diagnosis at face value (no “sure, we’ll do the brakes”).
  • Used East-West to book the appointment on the first offer.
  • Boom board / RO fully populated before vehicle enters the bay.
  • Customer separated from vehicle within 10 minutes of arrival (shuttle / loaner / lobby).
COACH’S CORNERAudit one RO per advisor per day.

Pick one finished RO. Read the documented concern as if you were the tech walking into the bay cold. If you cannot reproduce the customer’s symptom from the writing alone, custody discipline broke down somewhere in the call. Have the advisor re-do that intake aloud with you. Don’t scold — rehearse.

Inquiry-to-RO %
≥ 70%
% of phone inquiries that become an RO. This is the custody scoreboard.
Boom-Board Complete
100%
% of ROs with concern fully documented before work begins.
Separation Time
≤ 10 min
Time from arrival to customer being in lobby / shuttle / loaner.
